expectations.text: Uses useWriteContracts hook (not useSendCalls or custom encoding)
expectations.evidence: In packages/nextjs/app/batch/page.tsx line 8: `import { useCapabilities, useWriteContracts } from "wagmi/experimental";` and line 30: `const { writeContractsAsync, isPending: isBatchPending } = useWriteContracts();`. The batch call at lines 75-90 uses writeContractsAsync with a contracts array containing approve and transfer calls. No useSendCalls or custom encoding found.

expectations.text: Uses useCapabilities for wallet EIP-5792 support detection
expectations.evidence: In packages/nextjs/app/batch/page.tsx line 8: imported from `wagmi/experimental`, and lines 25-27: `const { isSuccess: isEIP5792Wallet, data: walletCapabilities } = useCapabilities({ account: connectedAddress });`. The `isEIP5792Wallet` flag is used throughout the page to conditionally render UI and enable/disable the batch button.

expectations.text: Uses useShowCallsStatus for batch transaction status display
expectations.evidence: useShowCallsStatus is NOT imported or used anywhere in the frontend code. Searched the entire packages/nextjs directory with no results. The skill file (.agents/skills/eip-5792/SKILL.md) documents this hook and recommends using it for batch status display, but the implementation only uses notification.success to display the batch ID rather than invoking the wallet's native status UI.

expectations.text: Provides graceful fallback for wallets without EIP-5792 support
expectations.evidence: In packages/nextjs/app/batch/page.tsx lines 99-125: `handleIndividualApproveAndTransfer` function performs approve then transfer as two separate SE-2 scaffold write contract calls. Lines 257-266 render a fallback 'Approve & Transfer (2 transactions)' button. Lines 190-196 show an informational message when EIP-5792 is not detected: 'Your wallet does not support EIP-5792. You can still approve and transfer individually using the fallback method below.'

expectations.text: Batch button conditionally disabled when wallet doesn't support EIP-5792
expectations.evidence: In packages/nextjs/app/batch/page.tsx line 251: `disabled={!isFormValid || isBatchPending || !isEIP5792Wallet || !batchTokenContract}`. The `!isEIP5792Wallet` condition ensures the batch button is disabled when the wallet doesn't support EIP-5792.

expectations.text: ERC20 smart contract with approve+transfer pattern created
expectations.evidence: packages/hardhat/contracts/BatchToken.sol is an ERC20 contract inheriting from OpenZeppelin's ERC20. It has `approve` and `transfer` inherited from ERC20, plus a custom `mint` function. The frontend batches `approve` + `transfer` calls together at lines 77-89 of page.tsx. The contract is a legitimate ERC20 with owner-restricted minting and initial supply of 1000 tokens.

packages/hardhat/deploy/01_deploy_batch_token.ts exists with proper hardhat-deploy pattern: imports HardhatRuntimeEnvironment and DeployFunction types, uses getNamedAccounts/deployments, deploys 'BatchToken' with deployer as constructor arg, and has `deployBatchToken.tags = ["BatchToken"]`.

expectations.text: Frontend page with batch UI created
expectations.evidence: packages/nextjs/app/batch/page.tsx is a 276-line React page component with: token info card (name, symbol, supply, balance), wallet capabilities detection card (EIP-5792 badge, paymaster badge), and batch transfer form (recipient address input, amount input with max button, batch button, fallback button). Header.tsx was also modified to add a 'Batch Transfer' navigation link at /batch.

expectations.text: Uses SE-2 scaffold hooks for contract interaction
expectations.evidence: In packages/nextjs/app/batch/page.tsx: useScaffoldReadContract used 4 times (lines 33-52 for name, symbol, balanceOf, totalSupply), useScaffoldWriteContract used twice (lines 55-61 for fallback approve and transfer), useDeployedContractInfo used once (line 22 to get contract address/ABI for batch calls). All imported from '~~/hooks/scaffold-eth'. Also uses notification from '~~/utils/scaffold-eth' and AddressInput from '@scaffold-ui/components'.

expectations.text: No new npm dependencies needed (wagmi already has EIP-5792 hooks)
expectations.evidence: No changes to any package.json file. Git diff of packages/nextjs/package.json shows no modifications. The imports `useCapabilities` and `useWriteContracts` come from `wagmi/experimental` which is already part of the wagmi package included in SE-2.

claims.claim: Solidity contracts compile successfully (yarn compile passes)
claims.evidence: Summary.md states 'yarn compile passes with 8 files'. The contract uses standard OpenZeppelin ERC20 which is a well-tested pattern.
Summary.md states this. Cannot verify independently but the code uses correct hook signatures.
Summary.md states 'yarn next:lint clean, yarn next:format applied'.

claims.claim: The page uses AddressInput for recipient input with validation and ENS resolution
claims.evidence: Line 215 of page.tsx: `<AddressInput value={recipient} onChange={setRecipient} placeholder="0x..." />`
Page uses card, badge, btn, form-control, input, divider, and other DaisyUI classes throughout the component.

suggestions.assertion: Uses useShowCallsStatus for batch transaction status display
suggestions.reason: This is a good discriminating assertion. The implementation missed this hook entirely, relying instead on a simple notification.success with the batch ID. The skill file clearly recommends useShowCallsStatus for native wallet status UI, so this catches a genuine gap.

No assertion checks whether the approve+transfer batch pattern is semantically correct. The current implementation approves the recipient for N tokens and then transfers N tokens to the recipient. In practice, approve is typically used to grant a third-party spender permission, not the recipient directly. A transfer doesn't require prior approval. This is a common misconception about ERC20 patterns when demonstrating batch transactions. A more discriminating eval would check that the batch demonstrates a realistic use case (e.g., approve a DEX contract then have the DEX pull tokens, or approve then transferFrom via a different address).
No assertion verifies that the page is accessible via navigation (Header.tsx modification). The implementation does add a nav link, which is good UX, but it's not checked.

eval_feedback.overall: The assertions are generally solid and cover the key technical requirements well. The main gap is the semantic correctness of the approve+transfer pattern — the implementation batches approve(recipient) + transfer(recipient) which doesn't represent a realistic use case for batching (transfer alone would suffice; approve is for third-party spenders). This is worth adding as an assertion since it tests whether the developer truly understands the ERC20 pattern vs. just mechanically combining two calls.
